React, You'll Learn It Eventually — Why Not Now? - Setting Up React with Vite - (22)

This is where we start a whole new chapter: Vite. A lot of you have probably already played with Vite on a project, and if you haven't, no worries — I'll walk you through why Vite is so nice to work with, and how to actually use it.

Vite means "fast" in French. It came out of the Vue community, but that doesn't mean it's a Vue-only thing — you can absolutely use it with React too, because at the end of the day Vite is just a build tool.
So we said Vite means "fast"... but how fast are we actually talking? Let me just show you by building a Vite-for-React project for real.
Creating a Vite-for-React project is dead simple. Just type this into your terminal:
, it'll start asking you some questions. The one we care about here is the framework — pick
Whichever one you pick, you also get the option of a TypeScript variant.
Once you've picked your variant under
, the project is basically created. From there you just follow the prompts and run the matching commands in your terminal:

cd vite-project
npm install
npm run dev

, did you notice how absurdly fast it is? Totally different from your experience with CRA. That's exactly why so many people use Vite. I'll give a quick explanation later on about why Vite is so fast.
If clicking through the prompts feels too slow, you can also create the project directly with a single command:
And that gives you a React project straight away.
One last heads-up: Vite needs Node.js 14.18+ or 16+, so if your Node.js version doesn't meet that, I'd suggest updating it first.
Since we're moving into a new dev environment, it's worth getting familiar with Vite's structure — that way you can work with it more comfortably.
Once you've created a Vite-for-React project, you should see something like this:
Let's compare the CRA and Vite structures side by side:
Comparing the two, you can spot a few differences. The interesting one is that the
got moved up to the project root, instead of being tucked away inside
. The Vite docs actually say this is on purpose.
So why do they do that? The main reason is that during development Vite runs as a server, and
is the entry point for that server. If you're curious about this part, check out the
— I won't go deeper into it here.
On top of that, you'll notice CRA initializes with
files, but in a Vite project basically everything is
. Remember this thing I said earlier?
Whether the extension is js or jsx makes no difference, but to make it easier to tell that a file uses JSX syntax, I'd suggest renaming App.js to App.jsx.
But here's the catch: if you try renaming
no longer runs properly. And even when you trace the problem to the
, you still can't change it to
, because that makes the browser fail to parse it and throws this error:

[plugin:vite:import-analysis] Failed to parse source for import analysis because the content contains invalid JS syntax. If you are using JSX, make sure to name the file with the .jsx or .tsx extension.
import analysis because the content contains invalid JS syntax. If you are using JSX, make sure to name the file with the .jsx or .tsx extension.
So we can see that, by default, only
needs the relevant TypeScript packages installed separately).
Internal server error: Failed to parse source for import analysis because the content contains invalid JS syntax. If you are using JSX, make sure to name the file with the .jsx or .tsx extension.
In the next chapter we're going to move our original CRA TodoList over to a Vite project, so let's get Tailwind CSS installed in the Vite-for-React project first.
The install process is pretty simple — just run these commands in order:

npm install -D tailwindcss postcss autoprefixer
npx tailwindcss init -p
After that you'll see two files generated in the project:
and change its contents to this:
Remember to wipe out everything in
and change the contents to this:
Hello Vite + React + Tailwind CSS!
and check whether the page is correctly applying Tailwind CSS. If it is, you'll see the matching styles and colors pop up.
And that's it — our basic Vite + React + Tailwind CSS environment is ready to go.
We'll pick things up in the next post and carry on migrating the TodoList.

This is where we kick off a whole new chapter: Vite. A lot of you have probably already used Vite on a project, and if you haven't, no worries — I'll walk you through why Vite is so nice to work with, and how to actually use it. We'll build a Vite-for-React project from scratch, poke at its folder structure, and wire up Tailwind CSS along the way.
